docs.title: Welcome to Goiabada
docs.text: Goiabada is an open-source OAuth2 and OpenID Connect server that makes user management simple and secure.

docs.title: What does Goiabada do?
docs.text:
If you're building applications and need to handle user logins, manage permissions, or enable users to sign in across multiple apps with one account, Goiabada takes care of this for you. Instead of building authentication from scratch, you can integrate Goiabada and focus on what makes your application unique.

Goiabada handles:

  • User authentication - Secure login with passwords and two-factor authentication
  • Authorization - Control who can access what in your applications
  • Single sign-on (SSO) - Let users log in once and access multiple apps
  • API security - Protect server-to-server communications
  • User self-service - Give users control over their own profiles and security settings

docs.title: Who is Goiabada for?
docs.text:
• Developers building web or mobile apps who need authentication
  • Teams wanting to centralize user management across multiple applications
  • Organizations that need to self-host their identity provider for security or compliance
  • Anyone who wants an alternative to proprietary services like Auth0, Keycloak or cloud-only solutions

docs.title: Key features
docs.text:
• Built with Go - Fast, lightweight, and resource-efficient
  • Easy-to-use interface - Simple admin console and user account pages
  • Single sign-on (SSO) - One login for all your connected applications
  • Two-factor authentication (2FA) - Extra security with one-time passwords (OTP)
  • Fine-grained permissions - Control access to resources with precision
  • Self-service account management - Users can update their own details
  • OAuth2 & OpenID Connect - Industry-standard protocols for secure authentication
  • Dynamic client registration - Apps can self-register (RFC 7591) - perfect for MCP servers and native applications
  • Multiple database support - MySQL, PostgreSQL, Microsoft SQL Server, or SQLite

docs.title: Why choose Goiabada?
docs.text:
• Self-hosted - Keep full control of your user data and infrastructure
  • Open source - Transparent, auditable, and community-driven
  • Lightweight - Low resource usage means lower hosting costs
  • Standards-compliant - Built on OAuth2 and OpenID Connect for maximum compatibility
  • Developer-friendly - Quick to set up, easy to integrate

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text:
Ready to try Goiabada? Check out the Getting started guide to get up and running quickly.

You can also explore our Docker images for easy deployment.

docs.title: About
docs.text:
Goiabada was developed to be an easy-to-use container for user management. Plenty of alternatives exist, but this is my personal take on the concept.

I'm committed to make Goiabada forever free and open source. I want you to self host it and have total control over the code and data.

The name is inspired by a Brazilian sweet made with guava. I was keen on choosing a word that started with Go 😉

Don't hesitate to contact me at contact@leodip.com, or consider visiting my blog or LinkedIn profile.

Leonardo D'Ippolito

docs.title: App name, issuer and UI theme
docs.text: The most basic level of customization is to set the app name, issuer (the same as the public deployment URL) and also choosing a UI theme. Those things should be customized in the admin area, using the web interface.

docs.title: HTML templates
docs.text:
You can also modify any HTML template of Goiabada, if you wish, provided you don't break the existing UI code (the javascript sections, element ids, etc.). You need to be careful and know what you're doing.

The overall strategy is:

  1. copy the web folder from container to host
  2. make your modifications in the HTML/CSS files
  3. relaunch the Goiabada container with a volume, mapping from host folder to container folder.
  4. set GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_TEMPLATEDIR and/or GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_TEMPLATEDIR to mapped folder. You can also set GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_STATICDIR and/or GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_STATICDIR to a mapped folder, if you want to serve static files from the filesystem.

docs.title: Tailwind CSS
docs.text:
Goiabada uses Tailwind CSS. When customizing the templates you can add/change Tailwind CSS classes if you wish. If you do that, it's necessary that you run the Tailwind CLI tool, after you finish editing the files. The Tailwind CLI tool will regenerate the file main.css that is used by the application.

You can download the Tailwind CLI tool here: https://tailwindcss.com/blog/standalone-cli.

Here's how you can regenerate the main.css file:

(the current folder here is one level up the web folder)

tailwindcss -c ./web/tailwindcss/tailwind.config.js -i ./web/tailwindcss/input.css -o ./web/static/main.css

docs.title: Development
docs.text:
Goibada was developed in Go using Linux (❤️). The github repository has a vscode dev container with all the dependencies pre-configured to run Goiabada locally.

To get started, simply clone the repository, install Microsoft's Dev Containers extension and open it in the dev container. You can make serve the authserver and make serve the adminconsole, from their respective folders. That will give you a running Goiabada.

For integration tests, first make serve the authserver to start it, then in another terminal use the make test-local script from the authserver folder, to run the tests.

Goiabada uses go-sqlbuilder for SQL generation, Tailwind CSS with DaisyUI for UI & styling, and the chi router to manage the incoming HTTP requests.

Bug reports and pull requests are encouraged. You can reach out to me at contact@leodip.com. Your involvement is welcome and appreciated.

docs.title: Environment variables
docs.text:
Goiabada consists of two separate applications:

  • Auth server (goiabada-authserver) - Handles authentication, OAuth2/OIDC endpoints, and database access
  • Admin console (goiabada-adminconsole) - Provides the administrative interface

Each application has its own set of environment variables and runs as a separate container/process.

docs.title: Configuration format
docs.text:
Goiabada can be configured using environment variables or command-line flags.

When both an environment variable and a flag are set for the same option, the flag takes precedence.

docs.title: Auth server vars
docs.text:
These environment variables are only used by the auth server (goiabada-authserver).

Since version 1.2, only the auth server accesses the database directly. The admin console communicates with the auth server via HTTP APIs.

docs.title: Initial setup
docs.text:
Used by: Auth server only

These settings are only used when the auth server starts for the first time.

GOIABADA_ADMIN_EMAILFlag: --admin-email Email address for the initial admin user. Default: admin@example.com GOIABADA_ADMIN_PASSWORDFlag: --admin-password Password for the initial admin user. Default: changeme GOIABADA_APPNAMEFlag: --appname Application name shown in the UI (can be changed later in the admin console). Default: Goiabada

docs.title: Database configuration
docs.text:
Used by: Auth server only

GOIABADA_DB_TYPEFlag: --db-type Database type. Supported options:
  • mysql - MySQL or MariaDB
  • postgres - PostgreSQL
  • mssql - Microsoft SQL Server
  • sqlite - SQLite (suitable for light workloads and single-instance deployments)
Default: sqlite

docs.title: For MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server
docs.text:
The following variables are used to configure connections to MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, and Microsoft SQL Server databases. These variables are NOT used when GOIABADA_DB_TYPE=sqlite.

GOIABADA_DB_USERNAMEFlag: --db-username Database username for authentication. Default: root GOIABADA_DB_PASSWORDFlag: --db-password Database password for authentication. Default: (empty) GOIABADA_DB_HOSTFlag: --db-host Database server hostname or IP address. In Docker/Kubernetes, use the service name (e.g., mysql-server, postgres). Default: localhost GOIABADA_DB_PORTFlag: --db-port Database server TCP port. Standard ports: MySQL=3306, PostgreSQL=5432, SQL Server=1433. Default: 3306 GOIABADA_DB_NAMEFlag: --db-name Database (schema) name. The database will be automatically created if it doesn't exist. Default: goiabada

docs.title: For SQLite only
docs.text:
The following variable is used only when GOIABADA_DB_TYPE=sqlite. When using SQLite, the variables above (USERNAME, PASSWORD, HOST, PORT, NAME) are ignored.

GOIABADA_DB_DSNFlag: --db-dsn Database DSN (Data Source Name) for SQLite. When using a file, include these pragmas for better performance and concurrency:?_pragma=busy_timeout=5000&_pragma=journal_mode=WAL.Example: file:/home/john/goiabada.db?_pragma=busy_timeout=5000&_pragma=journal_mode=WAL.Default: file::memory:?cache=shared

docs.title: Network configuration
docs.text:
Used by: Auth server only

GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_BASEURLFlag: --authserver-baseurl The publicly accessible URL where users and applications reach the auth server from outside (e.g., from browsers or external services). This URL is used in OAuth redirects, token issuer claims, and OIDC discovery documents. Example: https://auth.example.com. Default: http://localhost:9090 GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_INTERNALBASEURLFlag: --authserver-internalbaseurl Optional internal URL for server-to-server communication within the same network (e.g., Docker network, Kubernetes cluster). When set, the admin console uses this URL for OAuth token exchange and JWT validation (JWKS endpoint) instead of the base URL. This is useful when containers communicate using internal DNS names (like http://goiabada-authserver:9090) while external users access via a different URL. If empty, the base URL is used. Default: (empty) GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_LISTEN_HOST_HTTPSFlag: --authserver-listen-host-https Network interface to bind for HTTPS. Use 0.0.0.0 to listen on all interfaces (Docker/production), or 127.0.0.1 for localhost only (development). Default: 0.0.0.0 GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_LISTEN_PORT_HTTPSFlag: --authserver-listen-port-https TCP port to listen on for HTTPS connections. Set to empty or 0 to disable HTTPS. Default: 9443 GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_LISTEN_HOST_HTTPFlag: --authserver-listen-host-http Network interface to bind for HTTP. Use 0.0.0.0 to listen on all interfaces (Docker/production), or 127.0.0.1 for localhost only (development). Default: 0.0.0.0 GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_LISTEN_PORT_HTTPFlag: --authserver-listen-port-http TCP port to listen on for HTTP connections. Set to empty or 0 to disable HTTP (recommended in production if using HTTPS). Default: 9090

docs.title: Security settings
docs.text:
Used by: Auth server only

GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_CERTFILEFlag: --authserver-certfile Path to TLS/SSL certificate file (PEM format) for HTTPS. Required if you want the auth server to handle HTTPS directly. If empty, only HTTP is enabled. Note: In production, you can either configure TLS here or run behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Traefik, etc.) that handles HTTPS termination. Default: (empty) GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_KEYFILEFlag: --authserver-keyfile Path to TLS/SSL private key file (PEM format) for HTTPS. Must be provided together with GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_CERTFILE. If either is empty, HTTPS will not be enabled. Default: (empty) GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_TRUST_PROXY_HEADERSFlag: --authserver-trust-proxy-headers Trust reverse proxy headers (True-Client-IP, X-Real-IP, or X-Forwarded-For) to get the client's IP address. Set to true if using a reverse proxy. Default: false GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_SET_COOKIE_SECUREFlag: --authserver-set-cookie-secure Set the secure flag on cookies. Should be true in production when using HTTPS. Only use false for local HTTP testing. Default: false GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_SESSION_AUTHENTICATION_KEYNo flag Required in production. Hex-encoded session authentication key used for HMAC signatures to verify cookie integrity. Must be exactly 64 bytes (128 hex characters). Generate with: openssl rand -hex 64. Default: (empty) GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_SESSION_ENCRYPTION_KEYNo flag Required in production. Hex-encoded session encryption key used for AES encryption of cookie data. Must be exactly 32 bytes (64 hex characters). Generate with: openssl rand -hex 32. Default: (empty) GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_RATELIMITER_ENABLEDFlag: --authserver-ratelimiter-enabled Enable rate limiting for security-sensitive endpoints to protect against brute force attacks. When enabled, the following endpoints are rate-limited:
  • Password authentication (/auth/pwd): 10 requests per minute per email
  • OTP/2FA verification (/auth/otp): 10 requests per minute per user
  • Account activation (/account/activate): 5 requests per 5 minutes per email
  • Password reset (/reset-password): 5 requests per 5 minutes per email
  • Dynamic Client Registration (/connect/register): 10 requests per minute per IP address
Recommended: Enable in production environments for security. You may disable this if you have rate limiting at another layer (e.g., Cloudflare, nginx, API gateway), or for development/testing. Default: false

docs.title: Customization settings
docs.text:
Used by: Auth server only

GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_STATICDIRFlag: --authserver-staticdir Directory for static files. If empty, uses files embedded in the binary. Useful for customizations via Docker volumes. Default: (empty) GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_TEMPLATEDIRFlag: --authserver-templatedir Directory for HTML templates. If empty, uses templates embedded in the binary. Useful for customizations via Docker volumes. Default: (empty)

docs.title: Logging and debugging
docs.text:
Used by: Auth server only

GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_LOG_HTTP_REQUESTSFlag: --authserver-log-http-requests Log HTTP requests to console. Default: false GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_LOG_SQLFlag: --authserver-log-sql Log all SQL statements to console. Default: false GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_AUDIT_LOGS_IN_CONSOLEFlag: --authserver-audit-logs-in-console Log audit messages to console. Audit logs track security-relevant events like user logins, permission changes, and administrative actions. Default: true GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_DEBUG_API_REQUESTSFlag: --authserver-debug-api-requests Enable verbose debug logging for API endpoints (/api/v1/admin/* and /api/v1/account/*). When enabled, logs include full request and response bodies with pretty-printed JSON, headers (with sanitized authorization tokens), and timing information. Warning: This logs sensitive data and should only be used during development or debugging. Never enable in production environments. Default: false

docs.title: Bootstrap settings
docs.text:
Used by: Auth server only

GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_BOOTSTRAP_ENV_OUTFILEFlag: --authserver-bootstrap-env-outfile File path where bootstrap credentials are automatically written during first-time database initialization. This enables Goiabada's two-step bootstrap process for secure credential management. Two-step bootstrap process: First run (bootstrap phase):
  1. Auth server initializes - Creates database schema and admin user
  2. Bootstrap credentials generated - OAuth client credentials and session keys are auto-generated and written to this file
  3. Auth server exits - Stops with instructions to configure credentials
  4. Admin console fails - Cannot start without credentials (this is expected!)
After configuration:
  1. Copy credentials - View the generated credentials (requires sudo - file is owned by root): 
    sudo cat ./bootstrap/bootstrap.env
    Copy all 6 credentials from the bootstrap file to your deployment configuration:
    • For auth server: GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_SESSION_AUTHENTICATION_KEY, GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_SESSION_ENCRYPTION_KEY
    • For admin console: GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID, GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET, GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_SESSION_AUTHENTICATION_KEY, GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_SESSION_ENCRYPTION_KEY
  2. Restart services - Both services start normally with configured credentials
Docker Compose example: 
goiabada-authserver:
  volumes:
    - ./bootstrap:/bootstrap  # Creates ./bootstrap directory on your host
  environment:
    - GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_BOOTSTRAP_ENV_OUTFILE=/bootstrap/bootstrap.env
    # UNCOMMENT AND FILL IN AFTER FIRST STARTUP:
    # - GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_SESSION_AUTHENTICATION_KEY=<copy from ./bootstrap/bootstrap.env>
    # - GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_SESSION_ENCRYPTION_KEY=<copy from ./bootstrap/bootstrap.env>

goiabada-adminconsole:
  # UNCOMMENT AND FILL IN AFTER FIRST STARTUP:
  # - GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID=<copy from ./bootstrap/bootstrap.env>
  # - GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET=<copy from ./bootstrap/bootstrap.env>
  # - GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_SESSION_AUTHENTICATION_KEY=<copy from ./bootstrap/bootstrap.env>
  # - GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_SESSION_ENCRYPTION_KEY=<copy from ./bootstrap/bootstrap.env>
Security: The file is created with 0600 permissions (read/write for owner only). Parent directories are automatically created with 0700 permissions if they don't exist. Note: This only runs during initial database seeding (when the database is empty). If you restart without wiping the database, the file will not be regenerated. Default: (empty)

docs.title: Admin console vars
docs.text: These environment variables are only used by the admin console (goiabada-adminconsole).

docs.title: Network configuration
docs.text:
Used by: Admin console only

GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_BASEURLFlag: --adminconsole-baseurl The publicly accessible URL where administrators reach the admin console from outside. This URL is used in OAuth redirects when logging into the admin console. Example: https://admin.example.com. Default: http://localhost:9091 GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_INTERNALBASEURLFlag: --adminconsole-internalbaseurl Optional internal URL for server-to-server communication within the same network. Similar to the auth server's internal base URL, this allows services to communicate with the admin console using internal DNS names. If empty, the base URL is used. Default: (empty) GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_LISTEN_HOST_HTTPSFlag: --adminconsole-listen-host-https Network interface to bind for HTTPS. Use 0.0.0.0 to listen on all interfaces (Docker/production), or 127.0.0.1 for localhost only (development). Default: 0.0.0.0 GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_LISTEN_PORT_HTTPSFlag: --adminconsole-listen-port-https TCP port to listen on for HTTPS connections. Set to empty or 0 to disable HTTPS. Default: 9444 GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_LISTEN_HOST_HTTPFlag: --adminconsole-listen-host-http Network interface to bind for HTTP. Use 0.0.0.0 to listen on all interfaces (Docker/production), or 127.0.0.1 for localhost only (development). Default: 0.0.0.0 GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_LISTEN_PORT_HTTPFlag: --adminconsole-listen-port-http TCP port to listen on for HTTP connections. Set to empty or 0 to disable HTTP (recommended in production if using HTTPS). Default: 9091

docs.title: OAuth settings
docs.text:
Used by: Admin console only

Starting with version 1.2, the admin console authenticates against the auth server using OAuth2. The admin console acts as a confidential OAuth client.

GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_IDFlag: --adminconsole-oauth-client-id OAuth client ID for the admin console. This client is automatically created during database seeding. If using GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_BOOTSTRAP_ENV_OUTFILE, this value is auto-generated and written to the bootstrap file. Default: admin-console-client GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRETFlag: --adminconsole-oauth-client-secret Required. OAuth client secret for confidential client authentication. This is auto-generated during database seeding. If using GOIABADA_AUTHSERVER_BOOTSTRAP_ENV_OUTFILE, load this from the bootstrap file via env_file in Docker Compose or envFrom in Kubernetes. Default: (empty) GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_ISSUERFlag: --adminconsole-issuer Expected JWT iss claim for token validation. Must match the auth server's issuer URL. If not specified, defaults to the auth server base URL. Only change this if you've customized the auth server's issuer configuration. Default: (auth server base URL)

docs.title: Security settings
docs.text:
Used by: Admin console only

GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_CERTFILEFlag: --adminconsole-certfile Path to TLS/SSL certificate file (PEM format) for HTTPS. Required if you want the admin console to handle HTTPS directly. If empty, only HTTP is enabled. Note: In production, you can either configure TLS here or run behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Traefik, etc.) that handles HTTPS termination. Default: (empty) GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_KEYFILEFlag: --adminconsole-keyfile Path to TLS/SSL private key file (PEM format) for HTTPS. Must be provided together with GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_CERTFILE. If either is empty, HTTPS will not be enabled. Default: (empty) GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_TRUST_PROXY_HEADERSFlag: --adminconsole-trust-proxy-headers Trust reverse proxy headers (True-Client-IP, X-Real-IP, or X-Forwarded-For) to get the client's IP address. Set to true if using a reverse proxy. Default: false GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_SET_COOKIE_SECUREFlag: --adminconsole-set-cookie-secure Set the secure flag on cookies. Should be true in production when using HTTPS. Only use false for local HTTP testing. Default: false GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_SESSION_AUTHENTICATION_KEYNo flag Required in production. Hex-encoded session authentication key used for HMAC signatures to verify cookie integrity. Must be exactly 64 bytes (128 hex characters). Generate with: openssl rand -hex 64. Default: (empty) GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_SESSION_ENCRYPTION_KEYNo flag Required in production. Hex-encoded session encryption key used for AES encryption of cookie data. Must be exactly 32 bytes (64 hex characters). Generate with: openssl rand -hex 32. Default: (empty)

docs.title: Customization settings
docs.text:
Used by: Admin console only

GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_STATICDIRFlag: --adminconsole-staticdir Directory for static files. If empty, uses files embedded in the binary. Useful for customizations via Docker volumes. Default: (empty) GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_TEMPLATEDIRFlag: --adminconsole-templatedir Directory for HTML templates. If empty, uses templates embedded in the binary. Useful for customizations via Docker volumes. Default: (empty)

docs.title: Logging and debugging
docs.text:
Used by: Admin console only

GOIABADA_ADMINCONSOLE_LOG_HTTP_REQUESTSFlag: --adminconsole-log-http-requests Log HTTP requests to console. Default: false

docs.title: Session keys
docs.text:
Both the auth server and admin console require session authentication and encryption keys in production. These are cryptographic keys used to secure session cookies.

Why hex-encoded? These keys must be high-entropy binary data (random bytes) for cryptographic security. Hex encoding is used to safely represent these binary keys as strings in environment variables.

Required format: - Authentication key: 64 bytes = 128 hex characters (used for HMAC signatures) - Encryption key: 32 bytes = 64 hex characters (used for AES encryption)

Generation:

Use OpenSSL to generate cryptographically secure random keys:

# Generate authentication key (64 bytes)
openssl rand -hex 64

# Generate encryption key (32 bytes)
openssl rand -hex 32

Important: Do not use regular passwords or plain text strings. The keys must be randomly generated hex-encoded values for proper security.

docs.title: Database access in v1.2
docs.text:
Starting with version 1.2, only the auth server accesses the database directly. The admin console communicates with the auth server through HTTP API calls using OAuth2 authentication.

This means:

  • Database environment variables (GOIABADA_DB_*) are only used by the auth server
  • The admin console no longer needs database configuration
  • The admin console authenticates as an OAuth client to access auth server APIs

docs.title: Overview of Goiabada architecture
docs.text:
Goiabada is made up of three main parts:

  • The auth server, which manages key endpoints for authorization, token exchange, and authentication forms
  • The admin console, where you can change settings and manage user accounts and profiles
  • A database that stores all the data

Starting with version 1.2, the admin console now communicates with the auth server through HTTP calls, and only the auth server accesses the database. This new design provides better separation of concerns and improved security.

docs.title: Deployment options
docs.text:
Goiabada can be deployed in several ways depending on your needs:

  • Local testing - Quick setup for development and testing (HTTP-only, not for production)
  • With Cloudflare - Production deployment with Cloudflare handling SSL (recommended for Cloudflare users)
  • Reverse proxy without Cloudflare - Production deployment with Nginx/Traefik handling SSL termination (recommended without Cloudflare)
  • Kubernetes cluster - Production deployment on Kubernetes with ingress controller
  • Native binaries - Production deployment using pre-built binaries without Docker

docs.title: Distribution options
docs.text:
The easiest and recommended way to use Goiabada is through Docker containers, with images available on Docker Hub.

You can also find pre-built binaries on the releases page if you prefer to run without containers.

docs.title: Option 1: For a quick local test (HTTP-only)
docs.text:
⚠️ For development/testing only - NEVER use in production!

This setup uses HTTP without encryption and is suitable for local testing on your development machine.

docs.title: Choose your database
docs.text: Goiabada supports multiple databases. Choose one:

docs.title: MySQL
docs.text:
1. Download the docker-compose file (view file): 

    curl -O https://raw.githubusercontent.com/leodip/goiabada/main/src/build/docker-compose-mysql.yml

  2. Start the services: 

    docker compose -f docker-compose-mysql.yml up -d

  3. Access the admin console at: http://localhost:9091

  3. Access the admin console at: http://localhost:9091
